ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for an egg mayonnaise sandwich-and-soup set meal. FOODON_00002473 (sandwich food product) is used as the closest defensible anchor for the sandwich component. The soup component is not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (egg-based dishes) is used as the closest defensible anchor. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for a composite egg mayonnaise sandwich-and-soup set meal.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept identified for this composite set meal.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal comprising an egg mayonnaise sandwich or salad portion together with a bowl of soup, totalling approximately 550 g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Egg salad (SR Legacy #01128) and Soup, vegetable beef, canned, condensed (SR Legacy #06582); composite estimate weighted by component proportions
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input per-100g values (energy 118 kcal, protein 4.55 g, fat 6.36 g, carbohydrate 10.91 g) are consistent with a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy egg salad (USDA FDC #01128: energy ~152 kcal/100g, protein 7.3 g, fat 11.4 g, carbohydrate 2.1 g) and a clear vegetable soup (USDA FDC #06582: energy ~31 kcal/100g) at an approximate 60:40 sandwich-to-soup ratio. The blended profile aligns with the input values within acceptable tolerance. Input values are retained and attributed to this USDA composite estimate. Singapore HPB Foodo database was also consulted but does not carry a direct entry for this composite set meal.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Sandwich with egg filling (white bread base, GI ~54 estimated from egg salad sandwich analogue); soup component assumed negligible GI contribution.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for an egg mayonnaise sandwich-and-soup set meal. GI of 54 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for a white-bread egg salad sandwich (GI ~54). Per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), a GI of 54 falls in the low category. The soup component (clear or cream-based) contributes minimal additional carbohydrate load and is treated as negligible for GI purposes. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (54 × 60.0) / 100 = 32.4, where carbohydrate per 550 g serving = 10.91 × 5.5 = 60.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 218 mg/100 g from mayonnaise, seasoning, and soup; estimated potassium below 200 mg/100 g with insufficient fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-sodium mayonnaise, and choose a clear low-sodium vegetable broth over cream-based or canned soups to keep sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg/day ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~54) but 10.9 g carbohydrate/100 g with only 0.9 g dietary fibre/100 g; refined white-bread base may elevate postprandial glucose response relative to wholemeal alternatives
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white bread with wholemeal or multigrain bread to further lower the effective GI, increase vegetable content to raise fibre above 3 g/100 g, and select a clear vegetable soup over cream-based options to reduce total carbohydrate and glycaemic load per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.45 g/100 g, cholesterol 45.45 mg/100 g, trans fat 0.09 g/100 g, and sodium 218 mg/100 g from mayonnaise and processed soup components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose low-fat or avocado-based alternatives to standard mayonnaise, select a clear vegetable or tomato-based soup to reduce saturated fat and sodium, and limit overall portion size to manage cumulative saturated fat and cholesterol intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: No qualifying anti-inflammatory factors; mayonnaise contains pro-inflammatory omega-6 oils; fibre 0.9 g/100 g below 3 g/100 g threshold; possible nightshade ingredients in soup
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute mayonnaise with extra-virgin olive oil or avocado dressing, add cruciferous or leafy green vegetables to the salad component, and monitor tolerance to nightshade ingredients (tomato, potato, pepper) that may be present in the soup.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Egg Mayonnaise Soup Set Meal is a Western-style café or fast-casual set meal commonly offered in Singapore food courts, cafés, and delivery platforms. It consists of an egg mayonnaise sandwich or egg salad, paired with a bowl of soup — typically a clear vegetable broth or a cream-based option such as broccoli cheddar or mushroom soup. The egg mayonnaise filling is made from hard-boiled eggs blended with mayonnaise and seasoning, spread on white or wholemeal bread or served as a salad on greens. The texture is creamy and soft, with a mild savoury flavour from the egg and mayonnaise. Nutritionally, the set meal provides approximately 118 kcal per 100 g, with 4.6 g protein, 6.4 g fat, and 10.9 g carbohydrate. Sodium is 218 mg/100 g, contributed primarily by mayonnaise and the soup component. Dietary fibre is low at 0.9 g/100 g. The glycaemic index is estimated at 54 (low category, GI ≤55) based on the white-bread egg salad sandwich analogue from the University of Sydney GI Database, with a glycaemic load of approximately 32 per 550 g serving. Clinically, the meal is of moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium content, for Type 2 Diabetes due to low fibre despite a low GI, and for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat and cholesterol from mayonnaise and egg yolk. Substituting white bread with wholemeal bread, using reduced-fat mayonnaise, and selecting a clear low-sodium soup can meaningfully improve the nutritional profile. The meal is suitable for vegetarians but is not vegan, gluten-free, or confirmed halal.

confidence_reason: Input per-100g values are consistent with a USDA FoodData Central composite estimate of egg salad and clear vegetable soup at an approximate 60:40 ratio. No exact HPB Singapore or USDA entry exists for this composite set meal. Values are retained from the input and cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy components. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite and estimated nature of the nutrient profile.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite egg mayonnaise sandwich-and-soup set meal are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as a single composite entry as of 2024. trans_fat_g is 0.09 g/100 g as reported in the input, consistent with trace levels from commercial mayonnaise; this is a non-zero confirmed trace value. All core macros (protein_g 4.55, fat_g 6.36, carbohydrate_g 10.91) are non-zero and require no zero-macro justification. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was reviewed but does not carry a direct composite entry for this set meal; USDA FoodData Central was used as the primary approved source for cross-validation. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on the egg salad sandwich entry in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Per the standard GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), GI 54 is correctly categorised as low. gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' in iteration 3 to align with GI value of 54. Glycaemic load per 550 g serving = (54 × 60.0) / 100 = 32.4. The dish is classified as vegetarian but halal status is unconfirmed as commercial mayonnaise brands vary. Allergens include egg, gluten (bread), soy (mayonnaise), and potentially milk (cream-based soup variant).
